theme: weaver folding a tapestry to fit a narrow chest
seed_problem: novel composite (no single classic seed): cache/tile-fault minimization under a byte-capacity LRU replay
mutation: composite: instead of choosing only a memory layout, the solver jointly chooses (a) a rectangular row/col-cut tiling of the tapestry (space-filling-tile-layout) and (b) a per-tile compression level capped by the weakest-entropy cell inside that tile (per-tile-compression-choice); a fixed access trace is replayed through a byte-capacity LRU whose fault cost is the tile's own compressed footprint (capacity-fault-coupling), so compressing one region's tile only pays off by freeing bytes that let a DIFFERENT region's tile stay resident -- a cross-region externality invisible to any purely per-tile decision rule.
title: The Narrow Chest
strategies: trivial: fixed 16x16 uniform grid tiling, level 0 everywhere (no compression) -- the checker's own baseline, ignores entropy and trace entirely

greedy: same fixed 16x16 uniform grid tiling (the space-filling reflex), but per tile picks the locally-maximal compression level its own weakest cell allows -- correct per-tile logic, blind to tile SHAPE, so on tests where low-entropy patches are planted straddling the 16-line grid, most tiles get dragged down to level 0 anyway and the cache still thrashes

insight (strong): detect the entropy grid's true rectangular boundaries directly (a row/col is a cut iff it differs from its neighbor), so every tile ends up exactly homogeneous -- this isolates each low-entropy patch into its own small tile and lets every background tile compress to the maximum level, freeing enough byte-capacity for hot regions elsewhere to stay resident under LRU replay, which is exactly the cross-region externality per-tile reasoning cannot see
